bullets.text: Genomics = study of entire genome content, size, organization, and composition
Purpose: understand why genomes differ, how complexity arose, where all the non-coding DNA comes from
Bigger genome ≠ more complex organism = C-value paradox
Best correlation: log genome size vs. % protein-coding DNA → r² ~0.99
Protein-coding DNA = ~1.2% of human genome
Introns = ~30%
Retrotransposons + retroviral elements (LINEs + SINEs) = ~45% — biggest fraction
Simple sequence repeats = ~3%
DNA transposons = ~3%
Unique noncoding = ~25%
LINEs: copy-paste via RNA intermediate → LINE's own reverse transcriptase → new DNA copy inserted elsewhere. Up to 6 kb.
SINEs: same RNA copy-paste BUT borrows LINE's machinery. ~300 bp. Alu = main human SINE.
DNA transposons: cut-paste directly via transposase. No RNA step.
Simple sequence repeats: don't move. Short tandem repeats. Used in forensics/fingerprinting.
Gene family: gene duplicates → copies diverge over time → related but distinct genes. Example: α-globin, β-globin, myoglobin.
Processed pseudogene: mRNA reverse transcribed → inserted into genome. No introns, no promoter = silent dead copy.
Shotgun sequencing: shred whole genome randomly → sequence all fragments → computer reassembles by overlapping sequences.
BAC-based: map genome first → sequence ordered BAC clones one-by-one. Organized, slower.
Shotgun vs. BAC: shotgun = random, no BACs; BAC = ordered, map-first
LINEs vs. SINEs: LINEs encode their own reverse transcriptase; SINEs borrow it from LINEs
Bacteria vs. Yeast vs. Human: bacteria = circular/no introns/operons; yeast = linear/~6k genes/few introns; human = massive introns/repetitive DNA
lncRNA gene count tracks complexity better than protein-coding gene count
Gene family vs. pseudogene: gene family = functional copies; pseudogene = dead nonfunctional copy
Transposon jumping into a gene = disrupts function → mutation or disease
Retrotransposon expansion = genome bloat, contributes to genome size differences between species
Failure to suppress retrotransposons = genomic instability, insertional mutations
Processed pseudogenes = can rarely be "reactivated" if they pick up a promoter = chimeric transcript
Given a genome with 45% repetitive DNA and 1.2% protein-coding: what's the biggest fraction? → Retrotransposons, not introns, not protein-coding
Given two sequencing descriptions — one uses random fragmentation + computer assembly, one uses ordered cloning — which is shotgun? → The random one, no BACs involved
Given a nonfunctional gene copy with no introns and no promoter — what type? → Processed pseudogene (retrotransposed mRNA copy)
Retrotransposons (~45%) = biggest genome fraction, NOT protein-coding (~1.2%)
Shotgun sequencing does NOT use BACs
Log genome size vs. % protein-coding = strongest correlation (r² ~0.99), not raw genome size
lncRNA tracks complexity better than protein-coding gene count
Chromatin = DNA + proteins (histones + non-histones) — the physical form of packaged DNA inside the nucleus
Purpose: fit ~2 meters of DNA into a 6-micron nucleus + regulate which genes are accessible
Compaction levels: DNA → nucleosome (10 nm) → 30 nm fiber → loops → full chromosome
Core histones: H2A, H2B, H3, H4 — 2 copies each = octamer (8 total)
H1 = linker histone, sits outside core, seals entry/exit DNA, required for 30 nm fiber
Non-histone proteins = scaffold proteins, transcription factors — more diverse types than histones
Nuclear lamina = lamin intermediate filaments lining inner nuclear membrane — structural support + heterochromatin anchor
Centromere = alpha-satellite AT-rich repeat DNA — kinetochore assembles here
Telomere = TTAGGG repeats, 3' single-stranded overhang, stabilized by shelterin proteins
Telomerase = ribonucleoprotein: has built-in RNA template + reverse transcriptase activity
Nucleosome: 147 bp wraps around histone octamer → looks like beads on a string at 10 nm level
30 nm fiber: nucleosomes coil into solenoid, 6 per turn, H1 required to stabilize
Loops: 30 nm fiber anchored to non-histone scaffold proteins → ~50,000–100,000 bp per loop
Telomerase: RNA template base-pairs with 3' overhang → reverse transcriptase extends it (adds TTAGGG) → DNA pol + primase fill in other strand via lagging strand synthesis
Centromere: kinetochore assembles on centromeric DNA → spindle microtubules attach → chromosomes pulled to poles
Barr body = one inactivated X chromosome in female cells = condensed heterochromatin, silenced by lncRNA Xist
Heterochromatin: condensed, silent, late replication, at nuclear periphery
Euchromatin: open, active, early replication, at nuclear interior
Centromere vs. telomere: centromere = AT-rich repeats, NO single-stranded regions; telomere = TTAGGG, HAS 3' single-stranded overhang
Histones in BOTH hetero- and euchromatin — NOT exclusive to either
Homologous chromosomes: occupy DIFFERENT chromosome territories in interphase — do NOT pair up
No telomerase in somatic cells → telomeres shorten each division → cell senescence → aging
Telomerase reactivated in cancer cells → telomeres maintained → cells become immortal
Centromere deletion/mutation → kinetochore can't form → chromosome not pulled → aneuploidy (wrong chromosome number)
Lamin mutation (progeria disease) → nuclear lamina collapses → premature aging
Loss of H1 → 30 nm fiber destabilizes → chromatin more open → inappropriate gene expression
A somatic cell divides 50 times with no telomerase. What happens to telomere length? → Decreases each division → eventually triggers senescence. Mnemonic: somatic = shortening.
You're told a region of chromatin replicates late in S phase and is transcriptionally silent. What type? → Heterochromatin.
A protein has no NLS and is 15 kDa. Can it enter the nucleus? → Yes — freely diffuses through pore (<40 kDa threshold).
Heterochromatin is NOT only at centromeres
Histones are in BOTH hetero- and euchromatin
Centromeres have NO single-stranded regions — telomeres do
Telomere length DECREASES with age in somatic cells — NOT increases
Homologous chromosomes = DIFFERENT territories in interphase
Bioinformatics = using computers to store, retrieve, and analyze biological sequence data
Goals: find genes, predict function, compare sequences across species, infer evolutionary relationships
Main databases: NCBI (GenBank = DNA sequences, PubMed = literature), UniProt (proteins)
NCBI = hub: sequences, genes, articles, BLAST, alignments, phylogenetic trees
PubMed = journal articles — search by author, keyword, title
BLAST = Basic Local Alignment Search Tool — finds similar sequences in database
ORF = Open Reading Frame = ATG → stop codon, no internal stops, long enough to encode protein
E-value = statistical chance a BLAST hit is random. Lower = better match.
UPGMA = algorithm building phylogenetic trees by grouping most similar sequences first
6-frame translation of genomic sequence → find ORFs → compare to known genes
Gene function prediction: BLAST unknown sequence → top hits are known genes → infer same function
Nucleotide alignment scoring: match = +1, mismatch = -2, gap = -5. Sum all positions.
Protein alignment adds conservative substitution tier: match > conservative sub > mismatch > gap
Conservative substitution = chemically similar amino acid swap (Leu→Ile, Lys→Arg, Asp→Glu)
E-value: lower = better. 1.0e-80 >> 1.0e-4. 0 = perfect match. 1.0 = likely random.
Phylogenetic tree: align homologous genes from all species → count differences → group most similar → build branching tree
UPGMA: groups two most similar first, then next most similar, builds outward until all connected
Molecular clock: gene mutates at constant rate → count mutations between species → estimate divergence time
Similarity vs. homology: similarity = %, quantifiable; homology = binary, yes/no, shared ancestor
Nucleotide vs. protein BLAST: protein detects more distant relationships (amino acids more conserved than nucleotides)
Node vs. clade: node = single branching point = one common ancestor; clade = ancestor + ALL descendants
Rooted vs. unrooted tree: rooted has defined ancestral base using outgroup; unrooted shows relationships only
Conservative sub applies to PROTEIN alignments only, not nucleotide alignments
Bad E-value cutoff → false positives in BLAST → wrong function predicted → bad science
Using non-homologous gene for phylogenetics → tree is wrong → false evolutionary relationships
Molecular clock gene under selection pressure → mutation rate not constant → inaccurate divergence dates
Ignoring gaps in alignment → inflated similarity score → overestimate relatedness
Alignment given: A-CGTAATG / ATCGTCATG. Score with +1, -2, -5. Count: gap(-5) + matches and mismatches.
Two genes share 90% identity in BLAST. Are they homologous? → You infer they are LIKELY homologous — you cannot say "90% homologous"
E-values given: 100, 1.0e4, -10, 1.0e-80, 1.0e10. Which strongest evidence? → 1.0e-80. -10 is impossible.
Given a phylogenetic tree, find common ancestor of two species → trace both lineages back to where they first join = their node
NEVER say "X% homologous" — homology is binary
E-value is NEVER negative — -10 appeared as a trap answer
Lower E-value = stronger match — students flip this constantly
Conservative substitutions only in PROTEIN alignments
"Likely homologous" requires high identity + low E-value — you're inferring, not proving
Replication = copying the entire genome before cell division so each daughter cell gets a complete identical copy
Semiconservative: each new double helix = 1 original strand + 1 new strand
Rules: synthesis only 5'→3', always needs RNA primer to start, eukaryotes use thousands of origins
Helicase: unwinds/separates strands at replication fork
Topoisomerase: relieves supercoiling tension ahead of helicase
SSB proteins: stabilize single-stranded DNA, prevent re-annealing
Primase: synthesizes short RNA primer (~10 nt) on both strands — RNA not DNA
DNA Pol III: main synthesizer, extends from primer 5'→3', fast and accurate
DNA Pol I: removes RNA primers via 5'→3' exonuclease, fills gaps with DNA
Sliding clamp: ring encircles DNA, keeps Pol III attached = processivity
Clamp-loading protein: loads sliding clamp AND tethers both polymerases + helicase into one replisome
DNA Ligase: seals nicks between Okazaki fragments, joins 3'-OH to 5'-phosphate
Helicase opens fork → SSBs stabilize → primase lays RNA primer → Pol III extends leading strand continuously
Lagging strand: new primer every ~1-2 kb → Pol III extends backward (Okazaki fragment) → Pol I removes primer + fills gap → ligase seals
Clamp-loading protein coordinates both polymerases so they move as one unit — lagging strand template loops around
Eukaryotes: thousands of origins fire simultaneously → replication bubbles merge → full linear chromosome copied
Pol III has 3'→5' exonuclease = proofreading built in — catches its own errors in real time
Telomerase extends 3' overhang after replication to prevent chromosome shortening
Leading vs. lagging: leading = continuous, 1 primer; lagging = discontinuous, Okazaki fragments, many primers
DNA Pol I vs. Pol III: Pol III = main synthesizer; Pol I = primer removal + gap fill only
Sliding clamp vs. clamp-loader: sliding clamp = processivity ring; clamp-loader = loads it + coordinates both strands
Bacteria vs. eukaryotes: bacteria = 1 circular origin; eukaryotes = thousands of linear origins
No primase → no primers → no replication at all — neither strand can start
No ligase → Okazaki fragments never joined → lagging strand remains fragmented → broken chromosomes
No helicase → fork never opens → replication stalls immediately
No topoisomerase → supercoiling builds up ahead of fork → replication physically jams
No telomerase in somatic cells → lagging strand can't fully replicate chromosome end → telomere shortens each division
Given a replication fork diagram: identify which strand is leading (continuous, toward fork) and which is lagging (fragments, away from fork)
Clamp-loading protein is mutated and can't tether both polymerases. What happens? → Leading and lagging strand synthesis become uncoordinated — fork slows or stalls
A cell lacks DNA Pol I. What accumulates? → RNA primers remain unremoved, gaps in lagging strand remain unfilled, Okazaki fragments are never completed
Both strands are NOT synthesized continuously — lagging is discontinuous
Primase makes RNA not DNA primers
Clamp-loading protein coordinates BOTH polymerases — not just a ring loader
DNA Ligase seals Okazaki fragments — NOT polymerase
DNA is constantly damaged by replication errors, chemical exposure, and radiation
Without repair: mutations become permanent → cancer, aging, cell death
Each repair mechanism is specialized for one damage type — knowing which fixes what is the whole exam
DNA Pol (3'→5' exonuclease) = proofreading
Photolyase = light-activated enzyme that directly splits pyrimidine dimers. Humans lack this.
Alkyl/methyl-transferase = suicide enzyme for alkylated bases
DNA glycosylase = removes single damaged base in BER
AP endonuclease = cuts backbone at AP site in BER
NER enzyme complex = recognizes helix distortion, cuts ~25-30 nt patch
Mismatch repair proteins = scan for mismatches, distinguish new vs. old strand
Translesion polymerases = low-fidelity, bypass lesions when normal pol stalls
NHEJ proteins / HR machinery = fix double-strand breaks
Proofreading: pol adds wrong base → 3'→5' exonuclease backs up → removes wrong base → reinserts correct one
Photolyase: absorbs visible light → energy breaks covalent bond between dimerized pyrimidines. Direct reversal, no excision.
Alkyl-transferase: transfers methyl group from O⁶-methylguanine to itself → enzyme destroyed. One shot.
BER: glycosylase removes damaged base → AP endonuclease cuts backbone → pol fills 1 nt → ligase seals
NER: proteins sense helix distortion → cut strand ~25-30 nt on both sides → oligonucleotide released → pol fills → ligase seals
MMR: protein senses mismatch → identifies new strand (bacteria = unmethylated; eukaryotes = nicked strand) → excises segment → pol resynthesizes → ligase seals
Translesion: normal pol stalls → specialized pol swaps in → inserts base across lesion (often A) → hands back to normal pol. Error-prone.
NHEJ: broken ends directly joined. Fast. Can lose bases at junction. Any cell cycle phase.
HR: uses sister chromatid as perfect template. Accurate. Only S/G2 phase.
BER vs. NER: BER = removes 1 base (small damage); NER = removes 25-30 nt patch (bulky distorting lesions)
NHEJ vs. HR: both fix DSBs; NHEJ = fast + error-prone + any phase; HR = accurate + S/G2 only
Photolyase vs. NER: both fix pyrimidine dimers; photolyase = direct reversal using light; NER = excision + resynthesis. Humans only have NER.
Proofreading vs. MMR: proofreading = real-time during synthesis; MMR = post-replication patrol
Alkyl-transferase vs. BER: alkyl-transferase = direct chemical transfer, no excision; BER = removes and replaces base
NER defect → XP disease → can't repair pyrimidine dimers → massive skin cancer from UV exposure
MMR defect → Lynch syndrome → inherited colon cancer risk → replication errors accumulate in dividing cells only
No proofreading → error rate increases ~100-1000x during replication
NHEJ error → bases lost at break junction → frameshift or deletion mutation
HR failure in S/G2 → DSB cannot be accurately repaired → chromosomal rearrangement or cell death
A patient gets extreme sunburns and develops skin cancer young. Which repair pathway is defective? → NER. Which disease? → XP.
DNA pol incorporates wrong base. Two mechanisms to fix it? → Proofreading (immediate, by pol itself) + MMR (post-replication)
A cell is in G1 and gets a double-strand break. Which repair pathway? → NHEJ (HR requires sister chromatid, only available S/G2)
A repair enzyme transfers the methyl group to itself and gets destroyed. Which repair type? → Alkyl/methyl-transferase = suicide enzyme
Humans lack photolyase — NER is ONLY UV repair in humans
Proofreading = 3'→5' exonuclease NOT 5'→3'
NER removes ~25-30 nt patch NOT just 1 nucleotide — students confuse with BER
Pyrimidine dimers block BOTH replication AND transcription on that strand
Translesion synthesis is error-prone — it does NOT accurately repair, it bypasses
Nucleus = membrane-bound compartment housing and protecting DNA, separated from cytoplasm
Why separate: RNA processing (splicing, capping, poly-A) completes BEFORE export = quality control checkpoint
What happens inside: DNA replication, transcription, RNA processing, ribosome subunit assembly
Nuclear envelope = inner membrane + outer membrane (outer is continuous with rough ER)
Lamins = intermediate filament proteins lining inner membrane = nuclear lamina = structural support + chromatin anchor
Nucleoporins = ~30 proteins composing nuclear pore complexes (NPCs)
NLS = Nuclear Localization Signal = short basic amino acid patch on proteins destined for nucleus
NES = Nuclear Export Signal = leucine-rich hydrophobic sequence on proteins leaving nucleus
Importin (α + β) = cytoplasmic receptor binding NLS, escorts cargo to pore
Exportin = nuclear receptor binding NES + Ran-GTP, escorts cargo out
Ran = small GTPase. Ran-GTP = HIGH in nucleus (RanGEF there). Ran-GDP = HIGH in cytoplasm (RanGAP there).
NTF2 = reimports Ran-GDP back into nucleus to be recharged
Import 1: importin binds NLS on protein in cytoplasm
Import 2: importin-cargo complex translocates through NPC into nucleus
Import 3: Ran-GTP (high in nucleus) binds importin → releases cargo inside nucleus
Import 4: importin + Ran-GTP exit nucleus through NPC
Import 5: in cytoplasm, RanGAP activates Ran to hydrolyze GTP → Ran-GDP → importin released, free to recycle
Import 6: Ran-GDP reimported by NTF2 → RanGEF in nucleus swaps GDP→GTP → Ran-GTP regenerated
Export: exportin + Ran-GTP + NES cargo form complex INSIDE nucleus → translocate out → RanGAP hydrolyzes → complex falls apart → cargo released in cytoplasm
Ran gradient drives everything: Ran-GTP high in nucleus = drives export complex formation AND import complex dissociation
mRNA export: uses NXF1/TAP pathway — completely separate from Ran system. mRNA must be capped + spliced + poly-A tailed first.
Nucleolus: rDNA → RNA Pol I → rRNA → assembled with ribosomal proteins → subunits exported to cytoplasm
Import vs. export: import = cargo released in nucleus by Ran-GTP; export = cargo released in cytoplasm when Ran-GTP hydrolyzed
Nuclear bodies vs. cytoplasmic organelles: nuclear bodies (nucleolus, Cajal, PML, speckles) have NO membrane
Heterochromatin vs. euchromatin location: heterochromatin at periphery near lamina; euchromatin at interior
mRNA export vs. protein transport: mRNA uses NXF1/TAP, completely different from Ran-based importin/exportin system
Small vs. large molecules: <40 kDa pass freely through pore; >40 kDa require active signal-mediated transport
Importin mutation → proteins with NLS can't enter nucleus → transcription factors stuck in cytoplasm → gene expression fails
RanGAP inhibited → Ran-GTP stays high in cytoplasm → import complexes form but never release cargo → nuclear import jams
Lamin mutation → nuclear lamina collapses → nucleus deforms → progeria (premature aging disease)
NPC blocked → nothing in or out → nucleus isolated → replication, transcription, translation all fail
mRNA export blocked → processed mRNA trapped in nucleus → no protein synthesis in cytoplasm
A protein has NLS but importin is non-functional. Where does the protein stay? → Cytoplasm — importin must bind NLS first
Ran-GTP is experimentally depleted from nucleus. What happens to nuclear import? → Import fails — cargo reaches pore but can't be released inside nucleus
What is exported from nucleus vs. imported? → Exported: processed mRNA, rRNA subunits, tRNA. Imported: proteins with NLS.
A 200 kDa protein has no NLS. Can it enter the nucleus? → No — too large to diffuse freely, no signal for active transport
Importin alone CANNOT release cargo — needs Ran-GTP
GTP hydrolysis occurs in CYTOPLASM (RanGAP is cytoplasmic) NOT in nucleus
NLS does NOT need to be at N-terminus — it can be anywhere in protein
NLS is recognized by IMPORTIN not Ran, not exportin
mRNA export does NOT use the Ran system
Nucleolus is NOT membrane-bound — it is a nuclear body
Molecules exported = processed mRNA. NOT unprocessed mRNA, NOT DNA
